sources.title: 1 (Ethiopic Apocalypse of) Enoch - Online Critical Pseudepigrapha
sources.summary: Critical edition of 1 Enoch with introduction to Ethiopic manuscript tradition, textual variants, and relationship to Aramaic Dead Sea fragments. Based on Rylands Ethiopic Manuscript 23.
sources.content: The present Ethiopic text is based on Rylands Ethiopic Manuscript 23, which has also been reproduced in Knibb's edition. Rylands Manuscript 23 belongs to the family referred to by the sigla Eth. II or β. It was probably copied in the 17th or 18th century. Some of the most common orthographical errors found in late Ge'ez manuscripts, such as the confusion of the consonants ሀ, ሐ, and ኀ, አ and ዐ, ጸ and ፀ, are also attested in this manuscript.

evidences.claim: The Ethiopic text tradition preserves the complete 1 Enoch
evidences.corroboration: This source provides the critical edition base text from Rylands MS 23, showing the Ethiopic manuscript tradition and its orthographical characteristics
evidences.notes: Confirms Ethiopic as primary complete witness, translated from Greek

sources.title: 1 Enoch: An Update on Manuscripts and Cautionary Notes on Usage
sources.author: Larry Hurtado
sources.summary: Scholarly blog post discussing manuscript evidence for 1 Enoch, transmission history from Aramaic through Greek to Ethiopic, and cautions about using the Ethiopic text for reconstructing earlier versions.
sources.content: 1 Enoch is a composite text, the component parts likely written in Aramaic and/or Hebrew variously 3rd century BCE to late 1st century BCE. At perhaps various point it was translated into Greek, and then, sometime between the 4th and 7th centuries CE, was translated into Ethiopic. The only complete copies that survive are the Ethiopic manuscripts, the earliest of which are from the 14th century, but most of them later still. That is, about a thousand years separate our earliest copies of Ethiopian 1 Enoch from the time in which the translation was made.

evidences.claim: 1 Enoch was translated from Greek into Ethiopic between 4th-7th centuries CE
evidences.corroboration: This source provides the dating for the Greek-to-Ethiopic translation and notes the thousand-year gap in manuscript evidence
evidences.notes: Important caution about using Ethiopic to reconstruct earlier textual forms

sources.title: The Ascension of Isaiah: Translated from the Ethiopic Version
sources.author: R. H. Charles
sources.summary: Critical edition and translation of the Ethiopic version of the Ascension of Isaiah, including comparison with Greek fragments, Latin versions, and Slavonic translations. Published 1900.
sources.content: The Ascension of Isaiah imagines Isaiah's tour of the seven heavens, the descent of Christ through the divine realms in the form of an angel, and the death of the prophet at the hands of King Hezekiah's wicked son, Manasseh. This enigmatic work falls into two main parts: the martyrdom of Isaiah (chapters 1–5) and the vision of Isaiah (chapters 6–11).

evidences.claim: The Ascension of Isaiah describes Isaiah's tour of seven heavens and Christ's descent
evidences.corroboration: This source provides the complete Ethiopic text showing the seven heavens motif and the Beloved's descent through angelomorphic transformation
evidences.notes: Ethiopic version is most extensive witness to the complete work

sources.title: An Introduction to the Ascension of Isaiah
sources.summary: Scholarly introduction to the Ascension of Isaiah, discussing its composition, manuscript traditions in multiple languages, and the division between martyrdom and vision sections.
sources.content: The text we know as the Ascension of Isaiah is often divided by modern scholars into several major sections. The two major units are chapters 1–5, the account of Isaiah's martyrdom, and chapters 6–11, a visionary tour of the seven heavens, narrated in the voice of Isaiah. The most extensive text appears in Ge'ez (or classical Ethiopic). This text seems to have been translated into Ge'ez sometime in late antiquity.

evidences.claim: The Ascension of Isaiah exists in multiple language versions including Ethiopic, Greek, Latin, Slavonic, and Coptic
evidences.corroboration: This source confirms the multi-lingual manuscript tradition and notes the Ethiopic version as the most extensive witness
evidences.notes: Ethiopic version preserves both martyrdom and vision sections

sources.title: Religious Innovation and Sacred Scriptures in 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch
sources.summary: Scholarly article examining how 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch responded to the destruction of the Second Temple through eschatological innovation and reinterpretation of scripture.
sources.content: 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch, sometimes called sister texts for their shared congruencies, were composed around the end of the first century in Palestine by Jewish authors in response to the destruction of Jerusalem and its Temple. Both works were written in the apocalyptic genre with a narrative setting placed after the destruction of the First Temple. Both used a biblical figure associated with that setting: the scribe Ezra in Ezra-Nehemiah in the case of 4 Ezra, and Baruch son of Neriah, the scribe of the prophet Jeremiah in the case of 2 Baruch.

evidences.claim: 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch were composed around end of first century in response to Temple destruction
evidences.corroboration: This source provides the dating, provenance, and purpose of both works as responses to the 70 CE crisis
evidences.notes: Shows how both works reimagined covenant theology post-destruction

sources.title: 4 Ezra - Online Critical Pseudepigrapha
sources.summary: Critical introduction to 4 Ezra discussing its composition after 70 CE, textual transmission from Hebrew through Greek to multiple daughter versions, and its relationship to 2 Baruch.
sources.content: 4 Ezra was originally composed as a pseudepigraphal Jewish apocalypse, most likely in Hebrew, at some point after 70 CE. The original Hebrew version, which is now lost, was translated into Greek. The Greek version, which survives only in fragmentary quotations and paraphrases by later Christian authors, was translated into Latin, Syriac, Ethiopic, Georgian, Arabic, Armenian and Coptic. 4 Ezra is also closely related to 2 Baruch, which is normally dated to the end of the first or the beginning of the second century.

evidences.claim: 4 Ezra was composed in Hebrew after 70 CE, then translated into Greek and multiple daughter versions
evidences.corroboration: This source details the textual transmission history and confirms the post-70 CE dating
evidences.notes: Important for understanding the complex manuscript tradition

sources.title: The Evil Within - Advances in Ancient, Biblical, and Near Eastern Research
sources.summary: Academic article analyzing how 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch address theodicy questions through extended dialogues exploring human will, sin, and moral agency after the Temple destruction.
sources.content: Rome's triumph in the Great Jewish Revolt (66–70/74 CE) and the destruction of the Jerusalem Temple inspired the renewed flourishing of literary apocalypses in ancient Judaism. Fourth Ezra (2 Esdras) and 2 Baruch interpret the crisis and offer hope to the Jewish community in ways familiar to earlier apocalyptic traditions. Yet they also advance the apocalyptic genre as a medium of intellectual debate through extended dialogues that explore questions of theodicy.

evidences.claim: 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch use extended dialogues to explore theodicy questions after 70 CE
evidences.corroboration: This source analyzes the dialogic method both works use to address the problem of evil and divine justice
evidences.notes: Shows development of apocalyptic genre as medium for theological debate

sources.title: Enoch in Islamic Tradition
sources.author: Jafar
sources.summary: Scholarly paper examining Idris in Islamic tradition, his identification with Enoch, connections to Hermes Trismegistus, and his role as teacher and philosopher in Islamic mysticism.
sources.content: Islamic tradition preserves the narrative of a primeval prophet, philosopher, and teacher named Idrīs, a most enigmatic figure who is mentioned twice by name in the Qur'ān and whom Muslims have, throughout history, consistently identified not only with the biblical Enoch (Uḥnukh) – the most popular identification – but also with Hermes Trismegistus (Hirmīs), the Egyptian deity Thoth, the Roman deity Mercury, and the legendary Persian king Hūshang.

evidences.claim: Islamic tradition identifies Idris with the biblical Enoch and other ancient figures
evidences.corroboration: This source documents the identification of Idris with Enoch and explains the multiplicity of figures associated with him in Islamic tradition
evidences.notes: Shows syncretic development of Idris figure across traditions

sources.title: How Enoch Became Idris: The Co-Production of a Prehistoric Biblical Figure
sources.summary: Academic case study examining the process by which the biblical Enoch was identified with the Qur'anic Idris through borrowing, influence, and communal memorialization across Jewish, Christian, and Islamic traditions.
sources.content: Because the material is so far-reaching, the following essay will first describe Enoch's profile in the Bible and the Qur'an before turning to a selection of motifs associated with Idris in Muslim and Jewish literature. In the process, we see how the biblical past is made through borrowing, influence, and processes of communal memorialization. Idris is a particularly poignant example to think through the shared Muslim, Jewish, and Christian past.

evidences.claim: The identification of Enoch with Idris developed through cross-tradition borrowing and memorialization
evidences.corroboration: This source analyzes the process of co-production that led to the Enoch-Idris identification across religious traditions

sources.title: Idris (prophet)
sources.summary: Wikipedia article on Idris as prophet in Islam, his identification with Enoch, Qur'anic references, and traditional Islamic lore about his elevated status and teachings.
sources.content: Idris is an ancient prophet mentioned in the Quran, who Muslims believe was the second prophet after Adam. He is the third prophet mentioned in the Quran. Islamic tradition has unanimously identified Idris with the biblical Enoch. Many Muslim scholars of the classical and medieval periods held that Idris and Hermes Trismegistus were the same person. He is described in the Quran as trustworthy and patient and the Quran also says that he was exalted to a high station.

evidences.claim: Idris is identified with Enoch in Islamic tradition and described as exalted to a high station
evidences.corroboration: This source provides basic Qur'anic references and traditional Islamic identification of Idris with Enoch
evidences.notes: Wikipedia source with lower credibility but useful for basic references

sources.title: Angels and Angelology - Jewish Encyclopedia
sources.summary: Comprehensive encyclopedia entry on Jewish angelology, including detailed discussion of the four archangels Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, and Uriel/Fanuel as described in 1 Enoch and other Second Temple texts.
sources.content: On the other hand, Michael, Gabriel, Uriel, and Fanuel (Penuel) are introduced as the four angels of the face of the Lord. I saw on the four sides of the Lord of spirits four presences different from those that sleep not, and I heard the voice of those four presences as they gave glory before the Lord of glory: The first, Michael, merciful and long-suffering, blesses the Lord of spirits for ever and ever; the second, Raphael, set over the diseases of the children of men; the third, Gabriel, set over all the powers; and the fourth, Fanuel, set over repentance and hope of eternal life.

evidences.claim: Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, and Fanuel are the four archangels of the Lord's presence with specific offices
evidences.corroboration: This source provides the detailed description of the four archangels and their respective roles from 1 Enoch
evidences.notes: Authoritative reference for Second Temple angelology

sources.title: Uriel - Jewish Encyclopedia
sources.summary: Encyclopedia entry on the archangel Uriel, his role among the four chief angels, his association with fire and thunder, and his function as divine messenger in apocalyptic literature.
sources.content: Of the four chief angels, Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, and Uriel, who preside over the four quarters of the globe, Uriel is generally, but not invariably, mentioned last, although in this quartet his name is frequently replaced by that of another angel, thus showing the diversity of his nature. He is likewise one of the seven archangels, being the prince of the angels and of Tartarus. According to Kautzsch and others, Uriel is the angel of thunder and earthquake, and is, moreover, the divine messenger who warns the son of Lamech of the end of the world.

evidences.claim: Uriel is one of the seven archangels, prince of angels and Tartarus, associated with thunder and earthquake
evidences.corroboration: This source details Uriel's specific offices and his variable position in the archangelic hierarchy
evidences.notes: Shows fluidity of archangel names in Second Temple tradition

sources.title: Michael - Jewish Encyclopedia
sources.summary: Encyclopedia entry on the archangel Michael, his role as chief of the archangels, his position as guardian of Israel, and his functions in Jewish apocalyptic and rabbinic traditions.
sources.content: It is quite natural that, owing to his position with regard to the Jews, Michael should be represented in the Haggadah as the most prominent of the archangels. He is called by Daniel the great prince, and his greatness is described at length in later Jewish writings. He was one of the seven archangels first created, but among these seven four excel, and Michael is the chief of the four. Both he and Gabriel are called great princes; but Michael is higher in rank than Gabriel. He is the viceroy of God, who rules over the world.

evidences.claim: Michael is the chief archangel, guardian of Israel, and viceroy of God who rules over the world
evidences.corroboration: This source establishes Michael's primacy among archangels and his specific role as Israel's guardian
evidences.notes: Key source for understanding Michael's supreme position in angelology
